steps.title: Identify the claim Text 2 targets
steps.content: Text 1's underlined claim is that the stone tools "reveal that humans occupied the cave thousands of years before the Clovis people reached the continent." That claim depends entirely on one assumption: the crude stone pieces are human-made tools. Text 2 challenges precisely this assumption — it argues the pieces may have fractured naturally and that no other human activity or DNA has been found.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
Text 2 disputes whether the stone pieces are tools at all, not whether the connection to early humans is plausible or eventually provable.
  • Choice A — 'By suggesting that it draws a plausible connection between two groups of people but will need to be confirmed with further study': implies Text 2 accepts the connection as plausible — but Text 2 rejects the foundational premise outright, not merely as unconfirmed. ✗
  • Choice B — 'By asserting that it rests on an assumption about the stone pieces that is not sufficiently supported by available evidence': matches exactly — Text 2 argues the pieces may be naturally fractured rocks, meaning Text 1's claim assumes without sufficient evidence that they are tools. ✓
  • Choice C — 'By acknowledging that it will most likely be proved correct when the stone pieces undergo more detailed analysis': misreads Text 2 as supporting Text 1's conclusion, when Text 2 is skeptical. ✗
  • Choice D — 'By pointing out that it fails to account for evidence that the Clovis people were active on the continent as early as is commonly thought': introduces a claim about the Clovis people that Text 2 never makes. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
